summary: McKenzie shares a concise technical guide for setting up a Ruby on Rails development environment on Windows 7 64-bit. The post documents his solution after spending hours stuck in dependency issues, particularly around getting MySQL to work with Ruby on Windows. The key trick is copying a specific DLL file to Ruby's bin directory to resolve a common mysql_api.so loading error. It's a brief, practical troubleshooting post aimed at experienced Rails developers who found themselves on Windows.
key_points: Getting Rails running on Windows required navigating dependency issues that consumed hours of debugging
The critical fix was copying libmySQL.dll from InstantRails to Ruby's bin directory to resolve mysql_api.so errors
The post reflects the era when Rails development was heavily Mac-centric and Windows was an afterthought
money_quotes: Today I killed a few hours getting my Rails environment working on my brand new shiny 64 bit Windows Seven laptop.
I'm assuming you're a fairly experienced Rails developer and just ended in dependency purgatory like I did for the last few hours.
You'll get all manner of errors on that MySQL installation. That is OK.
You should now be able to successfully work with Rails as you have been previously, even from your Windows machine, and you will amaze your Mac-wielding friends.
key_insight: A brief troubleshooting guide documenting the non-obvious DLL copy needed to resolve MySQL dependency issues when running Rails on Windows 7.
